coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective fluorescence and smartphone-based sensor for detection of L-dopa using nitrogen sulphur graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Duhan, Jyoti
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: The level of L-dopa in the body has a significant impact on treatment of Parkison’s disease. L-Dopa, a precursor to the neurotransmitter dopamine has been unanimously recommended as the gold standard for treating the condition. The present work focuses on creating a flexible platform for the quantitative and targeted detection of L-dopa using nitrogen sulphur co-dopped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD). The NSGQD were prepared by using thiourea and citric acid via calcination process. The synthesised NSGQD emitted bright bluish green light with the addition of L-Dopa to NSGQD in basic condition the fluorescence of NSGQD was quenched. L-dopa is transformed into dopaquinone in an alkaline environment and photoinduced electron transfer causes fluorescence quenching. As a result, the intensity of the NSGQD fluorescence gradually decreased as the L-dopa concentration rose in wide concentration range and low limit of detection (LOD) was established to be 7.8 nM relative to other analytes, quinone derivatives and amino acids the NSGQD showed greater specificity to L-dopa. The sensing mechanism was thoroughly investigated using steady state, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption measurements and fluorescent studies. Additionally, this also proves through smartphones portable device using color detector software. Our research paves the way for the creation of an affordable biosensor that will work well for finding L-dopa in actual samples. Computational studies were carried out using density functional theory for better understanding the interactions of dopa with NSGQD indicating hydrogen bonds are formed between carbonyl group of dopaquinone with functional groups of NSGQD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen sulphur graphene quantum dots
L-dopa
Dopaquinone
Photoinduced electron transfer
Density functional theory
Smartphone sensor
Fluorescent

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen, sulphur co–doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD)
NSGQD optical, morphological and crystalline studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) Excitation and emission spectra of synthesised NSGQD (c) FT-IR spectra of NSGQD (d) the XRD spectra and (e) SEM image of synthesised quantum dots (f) AFM image of NSGQD.

(a) XPS spectra of NSGQD (b) C1s high resolution XPS spectra. (c)The high resolution XPS spectra of N1s (d) The high resolution XPS spectra of S2p (e) The high resolution XPS spectra of O1s (f) The histogram plots of zeta potential value of NSGQD, free dopa and mixture of dopa and NSGQD.

(a) fluorescent spectra of NSGQD with addition of dopa and (b) Stern Volmer plot showing quenching of NSGQD with increasing conc. of dopa (c) NSGQD fluorescent lifetime decay with or without dopa (d) Histogram selectivity plot showing selectivity of dopa rather than other species (e) The calibration curve using (R+G)/2 intensity of colors with conc. of dopa (f) Quenching of dopa in presence of interfering species.

(a) Optimized geometries of dopaquinone/NSGQD (C1) NH2-CS-CH2-GQDs (C2) COOH-CH2-CH2-GQDs (C3) NH2-CH2-CH2-GQDs. (b) Schematic representation of HOMO and LUMO energy orbitals of QDs and Dopaquinone and possible mechanism of electron transfer.
Schematic representation of synthesis of nitrogen sulphur co doped graphene quantum dots and its quenching in presence of dopa.
NSGQD fluorescence decay parameters from TCSPC with or without dopa.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective fluorescence and smartphone-based sensor for detection of L-dopa using nitrogen sulphur graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr. Br Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. Br Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr. Br Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The level of L-dopa in the body has a significant impact on treatment of Parkison’s disease. L-Dopa, a precursor to the neurotransmitter dopamine has been unanimously recommended as the gold standard for treating the condition. The present work focuses on creating a flexible platform for the quantitative and targeted detection of L-dopa using nitrogen sulphur co-dopped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD). The NSGQD were prepared by using thiourea and citric acid via calcination process. The synthesised NSGQD emitted bright bluish green light with the addition of L-Dopa to NSGQD in basic condition the fluorescence of NSGQD was quenched. L-dopa is transformed into dopaquinone in an alkaline environment and photoinduced electron transfer causes fluorescence quenching. As a result, the intensity of the NSGQD fluorescence gradually decreased as the L-dopa concentration rose in wide concentration range and low limit of detection (LOD) was established to be 7.8 nM relative to other analytes, quinone derivatives and amino acids the NSGQD showed greater specificity to L-dopa. The sensing mechanism was thoroughly investigated using steady state, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption measurements and fluorescent studies. Additionally, this also proves through smartphones portable device using color detector software. Our research paves the way for the creation of an affordable biosensor that will work well for finding L-dopa in actual samples. Computational studies were carried out using density functional theory for better understanding the interactions of dopa with NSGQD indicating hydrogen bonds are formed between carbonyl group of dopaquinone with functional groups of NSGQD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: L-dopa has been a typical medication for the cure of Parkinson's disease. More than 10 million individuals around the world are affected by Parkinson's disease, a neurological condition that usually disturbs the elderly persons [1,2]. Although the cause has not been established, a lack of dopamine in the brain is thought to be blamed [3]. The majority of the clinical symptoms were inflexibility, bradykinesia and tremors, which had a significant impact on the life of Parkinson's patients [4]. L-aromatic amino acid decarboxylase allows L-dopa to cross the blood–brain barrier, where it is transformed into dopamine and is stored in dopaminergic neurons [5]. As an outcome, it raises dopamine level in the brain that reduces the Parkinson's disease symptoms. L-dopa has a number of undesirable side effects that make it challenging to treat Parkinson's disease, arrhythmias, nausea including hypotension, confusion, random on–off and wearing-off [6,7]. In order to assess the patient’s condition and avoid the adverse consequences, L-dopa must be detected in a sensitive and selective manner.

Several analytical techniques for L-dopa detection have been described by numerous studies to date, including high-performance HPLC [8,9], electrochemical analysis [10,11] and optical techniques [12,13] using quantum dots and noble nanoparticles. These methods however, have a number of drawbacks including low sensitivity, protracted testing times and the need of expensive equipment and knowledgeable specialists [14]. Additionally, there are problems with the optical approaches because they often make use of heavy metal-based quantum dots or costly noble metals such as silver, gold and titanium to create noble nanoparticles in terms of cost and toxicity respectively. Due to its comparatively extremely sensitivity, low charge, ease of use and visual confirmation the fluorescence-based detection approach with non-metal doped quantum dots is given a lot of consideration.

When it comes to fluorescence sensing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) a zero-dimensional material, has recently gained a lot of attention, particularly because of their excellent photostability, photobleaching, biocompatibility and low toxicity [15]. GQDs have special photophysical properties that could create them a useful fluorescence probe. In the past, GQDs were fabricated using “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies similar to those of other nanomaterials. Graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes etc. can be used as beginning materials for the top-down approach [16–19]. However, the synthesis has many disadvantages like low yield, wide size distribution, lack of control over experimental conditions and techniques [20]. Smaller molecules are used as starting materials in the bottom-up approach [21,22] relative to a top-down approach, this strategy offers higher yield, better carbonation and more control over the optical properties. For fluorometric biosensing applications, the poor quantum yield of GQDs continued to be a limitation [23].

According to earlier publications, heteroatom doping can be used to adjust the intrinsic characteristics of carbon nanomaterials. Numerous dopants in GQDs including nitrogen [24–25], sulfur [26] and boron [27] have undergone extensive research and are found to have significant impact on the photophysical characteristics of GQDs. Fluorescence quenching of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots was employed by Chen et al. [28] to detect dopamine quickly and sensitively. Additionally, some studies demonstrated the effectiveness of nitrogen-sulphur Co-doped GQD as fluorescence sensing probes for dopamine [29], ascorbic acid [30], iodide and mercury ions [31] and detection of Levodopa sensitively and specifically using glutathione-decorated fluorescent CQDs [32].

Here, we describe the production of simple bottom-up nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD) for the highly sensitive and focused detection of L-Dopa. As far as we are aware, nitrogen and sulphur co-doped GQD have not yet been used for detection of L-dopa hence in this study, we outline the one-step calcination process used to create NSGQD and their response to L-Dopa in the sensory system. The advantages of our material is the synthesised materials are highly stable and detect L-dopa quickly with very low limit of detection till now not any sensor developed for L-dopa which have such an low limit of detection and for the best our knowledge the interaction mechanism are not studied with the help of DFT for L-dopa. The fluorescence of NSGQD responded a rapid quenching when L-Dopa was introduced (scheme 1 ). The quenching process involved an electron transfer from NSGQD to dopaquinone created by the oxidation of dopa in basic environment. A thorough investigation of steady states, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence was used to examine the hypothesised sensing mechanism. DFT was applied to study the interactions between dopa/NSGQD and also we prove the experimental quenching mechanism with DFT. We also developed smartphone based portable sensor for sensing of dopa with the synthesised quantum dots.

Melatonin, serotonin, epinephrine, norepinephrine and L-dopa were bought from sigma-Aldrich. Thiourea, citric acid (CA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets, HCl solution, glycine, alanine, and phenylalanine were obtained from LOBA India. All reagents, used are of analytical grade and fresh double distilled water was utilised to prepare the samples for all tests, dilutions, and analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen, sulphur co–doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: Water soluble NSGQD was synthesised through one-step thermal calcination of CA (origin of carbon), Thiourea (source of nitrogen and sulphur) using a previously published bottom-up techniques with minor modifications [33]. 2.5 g CA and 2 g thiourea was mixed and stirred on magnetic stirrer with 6000 rpm for 20 min so that a clear solution is obtained. Then allowed to heat up for 2 h in muffle furnance at 200 °C. When heated steadily, melted liquid changes colour from translucent to dark brown, signalling the synthesis of NSGQD. Additionally, the produced solid was immediately added to distilled water and stirred for the next 15 min. The solution was kept neutral (pH 7.0) and filtered before being used for purification with a dialysis bag of 3 kDa. Until additional characterizations, the resulting solution was stored in the refrigerator.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-F200, operated at 200 kVs), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SIGMA 500VP), EDS,AFM and X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab Xi + ), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Carry 630, Agilent Technologies) were used to characterise the structural characteristics and particles size of nitrogen and sulphur doped graphene quantum dots. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to find the zeta-potential. Measurements of UV–Vis absorption spectra were made for optical properties using a Carry Win UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, United States). The RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was used to measure fluorescence (Shimadzu, Japan). Time correlated single photons counting (TCSPC) of Horiba Jobin Yvon's (ex = 340 nm) was used to record time-resolved fluorescence spectra and Gaussian 16 were used for theoretical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NSGQD optical, morphological and crystalline studies
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared QDs were dissolved in double distilled water to make 0.0001 M solution. UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S1) of NSGQD shows absorbance peaks at 270 and 340 nm due to π to π* and n to π* transitions of benzene rings and C = O groups respectively. NSGQD had maximal excitations and emissions wavelength at 360 and 440 nm (Fig. 1 b) respectively [33].

Additionally, under 365 nm UV lamp, the colourless aqueous NSGQD solution appeared intense blue confirming its fluorescent nature. The existence of chemical bonding and functional groups in NSGQD was examined using FT-IR spectroscopy. (Fig. 1c) shows the FT-IR spectrum with broad intense peak due to the N–H and O-H stretching vibrations centred around 3383 cm−1 and 3190 cm−1. The peak at 3022 cm−1 appeared due to C-H bond of benzene ring and the presence of carboxylic groups is shown by the absorption bands about 1645 cm−1 and 1703 cm−1 and a small peak around 1430 and 1360 cm−1 indicates N–H, C–N, COO– bonds. The peaks originated at 1183 and 1093 cm−1 were due to C=S and SO3 - groups. The C-S bond shows transmittance at 733 and 948 cm−1. These results proove the successful doping of nitrogen and sulphur to the GQDs[34].

TEM images of NSGQD confirm the particles are spherical and size below than 10 nm (Fig 1a). ImageJ software was used to assess the particle size as shown in (Fig S1) revealing that the produced particles fall into the quantum dots range, have extremely low polydispersity and have an average size between 6 and 7 nm. Hence the synthesised particles are in region of quantum dots. SEM image of NSGQD also prove the spherical shape and nanoscale size of particles (Fig 1e). AFM images as shown in (Fig1f) shows that topographic height of NSGQD was lower than 2–3 nm that confirms the graphene like structure of synthesized materials[35]. EDS analysis confirms the presence of nitrogen, sulphur, carbon and oxygen atoms illustrating the successful synthesis of NSGQD (inset shows the selected area for EDS analysis shown in Fig. S6). The XRD patterns of NSGQD revealed good crystallinity and a large peak around 2θ = 23° (Fig. 1d) indicated the graphitic nature of synthesised quantum dots [36–37].

Elements and chemical linkages on the NSGQD surface were identified by XPS. Five unique peaks were visible in the complete scan spectra at energy of 162.8 eV, 230.2 eV, 292.5 eV, 399 eV, and 533.5 eV due to S (2p, 2 s), C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) respectively as shown in Fig. 2 a [38]. High resolution spectra depicted three peaks at 283.2 eV (C-C), 285 eV (C-N), and 287.6 eV (C-O) for C1s (Fig. 2b). The signal at 396.5 eV and 398.5 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectrum showed that N–H and C–N groups were present (Fig. 2c). The S2p spectra showed three peaks at 160.3 eV (C=S), 163.1 eV (C–S), and 165.5 eV (O–S) (Fig. 2d). The O1s high resolution spectra attributed to (C–O) 531.7 eV and (C-OH) 533.0 eV (Fig. 2e) [39]. These XPS results provided additional evidence that the functionalization of these GQDs with amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups as well as their effective doping with nitrogen and sulphur had been successfully accomplished. (Fig. 2f) shows the Zeta- potential value of NSGQD, DOPA and mixture of NSGQD + DOPA, we noted the surface zeta potential value of NSGQDs was −24 mV and for dopa the value is −19 mV and value changes to −13.6 mV when dopa is added into NSGQD which proves the electrostatic interactions between dopa and NSGQD [40] shown in table S2 and graph were shown in (Fig. S5).

The fluorescence intensity of NSGQD was observed by varying the excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm) and the corresponding emission response was measured. As the wavelength of excitation is raised from 300 to 360 nm [33] as seen by the fluorescence spectra of NSGQD in (Fig. S2) the intensity of the emission increases, however intensity falls off as excitation wavelength is raised further higher. The maximum intensity was observed at 360 nm and this was selected as the excitation wavelength. This type of emission behaviour is either directly related to the regularity of the size of the NSGQD or emissive sites are present in the newly established sp2 cluster. The pH dependence study is important for the sensing system because it strongly influences the production of dopa-quinone and also effects the fluorescence intensity of NSGQD. For this experiment, we prepared buffer solutions of pH from 1.0 to 12.0 and fluorescence intensity of NSGQD was measured for each buffer as shown in (Fig. S2). The PL intensity at low pH is small because carboxylic group on surface of GQDs get ionized and intensity is high in the range of pH 7–8 that decreased with further rise in pH [34].

After the assay was optimised, sensing research was conducted employing the fluorescence-based technique for the sensitive detection of dopa. The linear quenching of the NSGQD fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing concentration of dopa (Fig. 3 a). The stern-Volmer constant (KSv) is calculated with the help of fluorescence quenching plot using this relation shown in (Fig. 3b).
I/Io = 1 + [Q]Ksv

Where I and I0 is the fluorescence intensity of NSGQD in the presence and absence of dopa respectively at 440 nm and [Q] is the conc. of dopa. The limit of detection, limit of quantification, RSD and Ksv value was found to be 7.8 nM, 23.6 nM, 0.53% and 3.74 × 103 M−1 respectively in the concentration range of dopa (0–42 nM).

It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of NSGQD reduced with increasing conc. of dopa from 0 to 42 nM shown in Fig. 3(a) due to the formation of electron deficient dopa-quinone [41,42] in alkaline medium. The NSGQD is electron rich species as sulphur, nitrogen and oxygen is having lone pair of electrons and there is flow of electrons from NSGQD to dopaquinone [43–45]. The most probable fluorescence quenching mechanism concluded is PET (photoinduced electron transfer) (Fig S7). The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of NSGQD interact via -stacking, hydrogen bonding, coulombic interactions that effectively permit noncovalent interactions with amine, phenyl and diols present in the dopa [46–48].

Time correlated single photons counting (TCSPC) experiment was used to quantify the average fluorescence lifetimes of NSGQD in the absence and presence of dopa in order to further examine the exact quenching mechanism (Fig. 3c). Two general categories can be used to describe the quenching mechanism: dynamic and static. When a fluorophore and a quencher molecule collide, the photoexcited fluorophore transfers an electron to the quencher molecule that is colliding [49–51]. The addition of quencher concentration will reduce the average lifetime of fluorophore. Due to this excited state phenomenon, the fluorescence lifetime of NSGQD changed significantly demonstrating that the quenching process was dynamic. A non-emissive ground states combination between the quencher and fluorophore develops during static quenching [51]. Table1 contains the recorded NSGQD fluorescence decay data, both with and without Dopa, that were successfully fitted to the triexponential decay function. Average lifetime of NSGQD decreased with addition of dopa and fall from 22.52 ns to 14.8 ns respectively. It is possible to attribute the linear trends of the Stern Volmer plot and the significant decrease in the average lifetime of NSGQD with addition of Dopa. This proves the dynamic quenching process between NSGQD and Dopa [52–54]. Table S1 compares the current investigation with a few reports on the sensing of dopa in solution phases using fluorescent probes. The freshly synthesized NSGQD sensory performance for detecting dopa is shown to be comparable to some studies and provide fresh opportunities for developing the GQD-based detection techniques. Hence the NSGQD provide an effective technique for detecting L-dopa with high precision and reproducibility.

The detection approach was applied to confirm the very selective nature of the new fluorescence sensing mechanism developed for dopa as shown in (Fig. 3d). It was observed that NSGQD detects dopa in a highly selective and sensitive as it has reduced the fluorescent intensity significantly relative to glycine, alanine, tryptophan, melatonin, urea, phenylalanine, epinephrine, iodide, sulphates, sodium, potassium, calcium norepinephrine and serotonin. Fig. 3f shows the interference selectivity experiment.

Theoretical study for the interactions of dopa with NSGQD was carried out with DFT using basic set 6-31G (d,p) in GAUSSIAN 16 [55]. The substituted anthracenes with a range of functional groups, including –CH2CH2COOH, –CH2CH2CSNH2 and –CH2CH2NH2 [56–59] were computed. Optimized geometry of dopaquinone and anthracene with different functional groups are shown in (Fig. S3 and S4). With respect to this theoretical framework, we have determined the H-bonding interactions, HOMO and LUMO energies as well as the stability of the complex formed between dopa and NSGQD. Now is a good time to emphasize that the systems under consideration can be treated effectively with DFT by taking into account the experimental results that at basic pH, dopa will be converted into dopaquinone. Although this theoretical approximation has several flaws, nonetheless it provides a good representation of the molecule electronic structure, especially when complex chemical systems are involved [60–62]. In order to accurately describe the complex of dopa with NSGQD, three geometrical models were chosen (Fig. 4 a). These structures have been carefully simplified in order to prevent invalid implications. It is discovered that the three different forms of complexes primarily interact solely through hydrogen bonding between the dopaquinone(O) to the NSGQD surface ligand [63,64]. Table S3 lists the energies of all three complexes and also the bond length for interactions of each functional group with dopaquinone. It is found that more stable complex is formed when CH2-CS-NH2 of NSGQD interacts with dopaquinone as the bond length (N-H…O) is less(1.669Ao) as well as energy become less after the complex formation as if energy is less, more stabilized complex formed [65]. Our experimental results give us an idea of electron transfer from donor to acceptor species through PET mechanism that is supported using HOMO- LUMO with DFT calculations in GAUSSIAN16. HOMO and LUMO energies of NSGQD quantum dots are −5.16ev and −1.63ev and for dopaquinone are −6.52ev, and −4.07ev respectively. Energy gap of NSGQD is more than that of dopaquinone as is obvious from their band gaps of 3.53ev and 2.45ev respectively leading to successful electron transfer [66–68] from former to latter shown in Fig. 4b.

The portables and user-friendly smartphone-based imaging detection is more accurate than naked eye visual detection [69]. NSGQD solution-based nano sensor was developed using an internal android smartphone software. While the image was being captured by the camera, the android app simultaneously analysed it using the RGB-based (red, green, and blue colour space) software and the results were shown on the smartphone. Without using a flash, the photos should be taken with the camera as near as feasible to the sample vessel (around 6 cm away). The color change from blue to greenish with slow addition of dopa to NSGQD was visible by naked eye. Subsequently using the smartphone camera, RGB profiling was used to measure colour quantifications and the RGB values are shown in Table S3 [70]. B value decreased continuously and G value increased with regards to dopa concentration in range of 0–180 µM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9893. To create a linear relationship, we use (R + G)/2 as the abscissa and dopa concentration as the ordinate in order to further confirm the precision of the suggested smartphone detection approach [71]. We took the average value of red and green to decrease the errors and enhance the accuracy of results shown in (Fig.3e). Hence, utilising a smartphone equipped with the NSGQD nano sensor, simple and quick monitoring of dopa may be carried out. All of the aforementioned findings showed that the suggested smartphone detection approach could monitor dopa with sensitivity.

In conclusion, the synthesised fluorescent graphene quantum dots co-doped with sulphur and nitrogen are highly selective and sensitive for the detection of dopa. The sequential injection of dopa effectively quenched the NSGQD fluorescent emissions. The fluorescent detection of dopa had a linear range of 0 to 42 nM and a LOD of 7.8 nM. It has been established that fluorescence reduction of the fluorophore is caused by electron transfer from co-doped quantum dots to dopa-quinone. TCS-PC studies prove the dynamic quenching process between NSGQD and dopa due to substantial decrease in the average lifetime. We used DFT to better understand how dopa interacts with NSGQD nanoparticles. Hydrogen bonds are formed when the(=O) moiety of dopaquinone interacts with the NSGQD surface ligand. Our experimental results give us an idea of electron transfer from donor to acceptor species through PET mechanism that is also supported by calculating band gaps of HOMO-LUMO with DFT in GAUSSIAN16. An internal android smartphone software was also used for effective detection of dopa with NSGQD based nano sensor. This approach is inexpensive, simple and provided appreciable results and therefore it could have many practical uses.
Jyoti Duhan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sangeeta Obrai: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109262.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots from PEG for highly sensitive detection of levodopa
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Hao, Juan Liu, Sijie Guo, Yalin Zhang, Hui Huang, Yang Liu, and Zhenhui Kang. “Carbon dots from PEG for highly sensitive detection of levodopa.” Journal of Materials Chemistry B 3, no. 11 (2015): 2378-2387.

J. Neurochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sveinbjornsdottir, The clinical symptoms of Parkinson’s disease, J. Neurochem. 139 (2016) 318e324.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parkinson disease
Nat. Rev. Dis. Primers

Biosens. Bioelectron.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase activity: implications for L-DOPA treatment in Parkinson's disease
CNS Neurosci. Ther.

Postgrad. Med. J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitation of levodopa and carbidopa in rat plasma by LC–MS/MS: The key role of ion-pairing reversed-phase chromatography
J. Chromatogr. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chi, Jingduan, Yonghua Ling, Rand Jenkins, and Fumin Li. “Quantitation of levodopa and carbidopa in rat plasma by LC–MS/MS: The key role of ion-pairing reversed-phase chromatography.” Journal of Chromatography B 1054 (2017): 1-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodeposition of quercetin at a multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified glassy carbon electrode as a novel and efficient voltammetric sensor for simultaneous determination of levodopa, uric acid and tyramine
